key_regimens.cycle_days: AC every 14 days x 4, then Paclitaxel every 14 days x 4. All cycles with G-CSF support.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting, alopecia, neuropathy, cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin), fatigue.

key_regimens.name: TCHP (Docetaxel/Carboplatin + Trastuzumab + Pertuzumab)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, diarrhea, nausea, fatigue, cardiotoxicity (monitor LVEF).

key_regimens.name: Ribociclib + Letrozole (First-line mBC)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, hepatotoxicity, QTc prolongation, nausea, fatigue.

key_regimens.name: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (Second-line HER2+)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: ILD/pneumonitis (requires monitoring), neutropenia, nausea, fatigue, anemia.

key_regimens.name: Sacituzumab govitecan-hziy + Pembrolizumab (First-line TNBC)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, diarrhea, nausea, alopecia, anemia, hepatotoxicity.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Principles of Monitoring Metastatic Disease
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Baseline prior to initiating new therapy, then periodically during treatment (every 1-3 months for symptoms/exam, every 2-6 months for imaging) and as clinically indicated for progression.
response_logic: Response is classified as: response/continued response, stable disease, uncertainty, or progression of disease.
Progression is defined by unequivocal evidence of worsening disease at known sites or new metastatic sites.
Use widely accepted criteria such as RECIST or WHO for reporting response, stability, and progression.
The same method of assessment should be used over time (e.g., repeat CT scan for an abnormality initially found on CT).
Sometimes information from different sources (symptoms, labs, imaging) may be conflicting; clinical judgment and patient input are critical.
imaging_recommendations: For chemotherapy: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 2-4 cycles.
For endocrine therapy alone or with CDK4/6 inhibitor: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 2-6 months.
Bone scan every 4-6 cycles (chemo) or every 2-6 months (endocrine).
PET/CT: As clinically indicated (useful when standard imaging equivocal).
Brain MRI with contrast if CNS symptoms suspected.
In patients with long-term stable disease, the frequency of monitoring can be reduced.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy of first recurrence is recommended to confirm diagnosis and re-assess biomarker status (HR, HER2).
Re-biopsy should be considered at progression, especially if results will change management.
Tissue or plasma-based ctDNA assays may be used for genomic profiling to identify actionable alterations.

For progressive disease, treatment options include switching to an alternative line of systemic therapy, clinical trial enrollment, or transitioning to best supportive care/palliative care based on goals and performance status.

prognosis.overall: Breast cancer is the most common malignancy in females in the United States and is second only to lung cancer as a cause of cancer death. The American Cancer Society has estimated that 313,510 Americans will be diagnosed with breast cancer and 42,780 will die of disease in the United States in 2024 [1,2]. Prognosis varies significantly by tumor biology (HR status, HER2 expression), stage at diagnosis, and genomic profile. Patients with HR-positive, HER2-negative tumors generally have the most favorable long-term outcomes due to the availability of effective endocrine therapies. Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) is associated with the most aggressive clinical course and highest risk of early recurrence. HER2-positive disease, historically associated with poor prognosis, has been transformed by targeted therapies, with median overall survival exceeding 5 years for metastatic disease in the modern treatment era. Achieving pathologic complete response (pCR) to preoperative systemic therapy is associated with favorable disease-free and OS, particularly for TNBC and HER2-positive subtypes [255-257].

by_stage.context: DCIS is a noninvasive lesion. Approximately 50% of local recurrences after initial treatment for pure DCIS are invasive in nature, while the remainder recur as pure DCIS. Whole breast RT following BCS reduces ipsilateral breast tumor recurrence (IBTR) rates for all DCIS by 50%-70% [DCIS-1 footnote n].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: 90-99% overall survival for early-stage invasive breast cancer
by_stage.context: Multiple randomized trials document that mastectomy is equivalent to BCT (BCS with WBRT) with respect to OS as primary treatment for the majority of patients with stage I and stage II breast cancers (category 1) [113-117].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Variable depending on substage and response to preoperative therapy
by_stage.context: Preoperative systemic therapy is indicated for inoperable breast cancer including inflammatory breast cancer, bulky cN2 axillary nodes, cN3 nodal disease, and cT4 tumors. Data from numerous studies in patients with stage III disease suggest that postoperative RT improves local control even for patients who have a pCR to neoadjuvant chemotherapy [205-208].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Median OS ranges from approximately 20-40+ months depending on subtype and line of therapy
by_stage.context: The primary treatment approach for metastatic breast cancer with an intact primary tumor is systemic therapy, with consideration of surgery after initial systemic treatment for those requiring palliation. Treatment is guided by tumor biology including HR and HER2 status, genomic profiling, and germline BRCA1/2 status.

prognostic_factors: Histologic grade (Nottingham combined histologic grade: G1-G3)
Estrogen receptor (ER) status: patients with 1%-100% ER IHC staining are considered ER+ and eligible for endocrine therapies
HER2 (ERBB2) status by IHC and/or ISH
Genomic profile (21-gene recurrence score, 70-gene MammaPrint, others)
Germline BRCA1/2 pathogenic variants (approximately 5% of all breast cancer patients carry these)
Pathologic response to preoperative systemic therapy (RCB score)
Residual cancer burden (RCB) after neoadjuvant chemotherapy, which is prognostic within each breast cancer subtype [275]
prognosis.prognostic_context: The risk of recurrence between years 5 and 20 is considerable in patients treated with initial 5 years of endocrine therapy [318]. For patients with HR-positive, HER2-negative disease, gene expression assays such as the 21-gene assay (Oncotype DX) provide prognostic information by predicting distant recurrence risk and predictive information regarding chemotherapy benefit. Among patients with pN0, HR-positive, HER2-negative tumors with a recurrence score between 0-10, the risk of distant recurrence is low (<4%) and these patients derive no incremental benefit from adjuvant chemotherapy. Those with a recurrence score ≥31 have a higher risk of distant recurrence and derive significant benefit from adjuvant chemotherapy [286,352,353]. For node-positive disease, the RxPONDER trial demonstrated that premenopausal women with hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative, node-positive breast cancer and a recurrence score up to 25 benefited from adjuvant chemotherapy, while postmenopausal women did not [358].

disease_related.management: Educate patients on lymphedema, monitor for lymphedema, and refer for lymphedema management as needed. Consider pretreatment measurement of both arms as a baseline for patients with risk factors for lymphedema. See NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship: Lymphedema [438,439]. Factors associated with increased risk include extent of axillary surgery, axillary radiation, infection, and patient obesity.

Treat with denosumab, zoledronic acid, or pamidronate (category 1) in addition to systemic therapy if bone metastasis is present, expected survival is ≥3 months, and renal function is adequate. Patients should undergo a dental examination with preventive dentistry prior to initiation of this therapy. The optimal schedule for zoledronic acid is every 12 weeks [487-489]. Three randomized trials showed that zoledronic acid administered once every 12 weeks versus once every 4 weeks does not compromise efficacy.

Risk factor for bisphosphonate and denosumab therapy. Poor baseline dental health or dental procedures during treatment are known risk factors. A dental examination with preventive dental intervention is recommended prior to treatment with IV bisphosphonate or denosumab. During treatment, avoid invasive dental procedures if possible. Additional risk factors include administration of chemotherapy or corticosteroids and poor oral hygiene with periodontal disease and dental abscess [493].

disease_related.complication: Bone health deterioration during aromatase inhibitor therapy
disease_related.management: Baseline assessment of bone density is recommended for patients receiving an aromatase inhibitor who are at risk of osteoporosis (eg, age >65, family history, chronic steroids). Monitor bone density with periodic DEXA scans (every 1-2 years). The use of a bisphosphonate (PO/IV) or denosumab is acceptable to maintain or improve bone mineral density and reduce risk of fractures in postmenopausal patients receiving adjuvant aromatase inhibitor therapy [BINV-K footnote q].

Assess for distress using NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A). Survivors are at elevated risk for fear of recurrence, distress, anxiety, and depression that may persist for many years after diagnosis. Periodic screening and referral to mental health professionals if needed are recommended [BINV-17].

Cardiac monitoring is recommended for patients who received left-sided RT, anthracyclines, or HER2-targeted therapy. Increased cardiac toxicity has been observed in patients treated with trastuzumab and is further increased with anthracycline and taxane-based regimens in combination with HER2-targeted agents [412,425-427].

Hot flashes managed with venlafaxine (SNRI) or other agents. CYP2D6 inhibitor SSRIs (eg, paroxetine, fluoxetine) should be avoided in patients on tamoxifen as they decrease endoxifen levels; mild CYP2D6 inhibitors such as citalopram, escitalopram, sertraline, and venlafaxine appear to have minimal effect on tamoxifen metabolism [330,336-339]. The Panel does not recommend CYP2D6 testing as a tool to determine optimal adjuvant endocrine strategy.

chemotherapy: Peripheral neuropathy: Consider cryotherapy of hands and feet to decrease risk when receiving taxane-containing regimens. Use of glove constriction may also be considered [MICHEL LL, 2025;11:408-415].
Chemotherapy-induced alopecia: Consider scalp cooling to reduce incidence; results may be less effective with anthracycline-containing regimens [601-606].
Myelosuppression: All dose-dense regimens require myeloid growth factor support per NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors [BINV-M footnote n].
Nausea/vomiting: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis.
immunotherapy: Immune checkpoint inhibitor-related toxicities: Screen for and manage immunotherapy-related toxicities and endocrine dysfunctions (eg, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency) in patients treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors. See NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [BINV-M 1 of 10].

Pneumonitis: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is associated with ILD/pneumonitis. Regular monitoring for this serious side effect is recommended. For patients with a history of ILD/pneumonitis, there are no data on managing safety or toxicity of this drug in a trial [BINV-Q footnote o].
targeted_therapy: Cardiac toxicity: Trastuzumab is associated with cardiac dysfunction. Evaluate LVEF prior to and during treatment. The FDA label recommends LVEF measurements prior to initiation of trastuzumab and every 3 months during therapy [BINV-M 1 of 10].
Concurrent use of trastuzumab and pertuzumab with an anthracycline should be avoided due to cardiac toxicity risk.
Tucatinib: Common grade ≥3 adverse events include palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (13% vs 9%), diarrhea (13% vs 9%), elevated ALT/AST levels [HER2CLIMB trial].
surgery: Wound healing complications: Increased rates associated with smoking and obesity (WHO Class 2 and 3) [BINV-H 5 of 7].
Lymphedema: Following axillary surgery, educate, monitor, and refer for lymphedema management [BINV-17].
Breast implant complications: Capsular contracture, malposition, poor cosmesis, and implant exposure significantly increased if RT is administered to an implant [BINV-H footnote f].
BIA-ALCL and BIA-SCC: Rare implant-associated malignancies. BIA-ALCL commonly presents with delayed periprosthetic effusion and breast asymmetry occurring >1 year after implantation [BINV-H 6 of 7].
radiation: Normal tissue effects: Radiation-related effects to normal breast tissue such as breast shrinkage, telangiectasia, and breast edema may occur [173].

Cardiac and lung toxicity: Routine inclusion of internal mammary nodes remains controversial due to associated cardiac and lung toxicities. Meticulous treatment planning with normal tissue dose constraints is mandatory [BINV-I 2 of 3].
Cosmetic impact: The BIG 3-07/TROG 07.01 trial showed cosmetic status was impacted negatively with RT boost versus no boost after 2 years [35].

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is an integral component of breast cancer management across all disease stages. The NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A) provides the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health, for assessing patient distress at initial diagnosis and throughout the disease course [DCIS-1 footnote f, BINV-1 footnote h]. Patients should receive multidisciplinary and interprofessional care that seeks to prevent and/or manage morbidities associated with breast cancer or its treatment. Coordination between primary care providers and specialists is essential to ensure continuity of care. NCCN recognizes the importance of clinical trials and encourages participation when applicable and available.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Evidence suggests that a healthy lifestyle may lead to better breast cancer outcomes. A prospective study of 1,490 patients diagnosed with stage I-III breast cancer showed an association between high fruit and vegetable consumption, physical activity, and improved survivorship, regardless of obesity [444]. The NCCN Panel recommends an active lifestyle and ideal body weight (BMI 20-25) for optimal overall health and breast cancer outcomes [BINV-17]. Calcium (1200-1500 mg/day) and vitamin D3 (400-800 IU/day) supplementation should be provided to patients on bisphosphonates, with dose individualized and adjusted upward when vitamin D deficiency is found [BINV-21 footnote ppp].
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis for management of nausea and vomiting associated with chemotherapy. For pregnant patients with breast cancer, ondansetron and metoclopramide are considered safe, while NK1 inhibitors should be avoided due to insufficient human data [PREG-2].
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Myeloid growth factor support is required for all dose-dense chemotherapy regimens per NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors [BINV-M footnote n]. G-CSF use during pregnancy should be used with caution due to insufficient data, though it appears to be safe [PREG-2].
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Tamoxifen is associated with increased risk for deep venous thrombosis. VTE risk should be considered when combining abemaciclib with tamoxifen [BINV-K footnote f]. Patients should be screened for personal or familial thrombophilia, which may result in reconstructive failure of microsurgical reconstruction or thromboembolic events [BINV-H 6 of 7].
supportive_care.pain_management: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain. Bone metastases are a common cause of pain in breast cancer and should be managed with bisphosphonates or denosumab in addition to systemic therapy and appropriate analgesics [BINV-21].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Survivors are at elevated risk for fear of recurrence, distress, anxiety, and depression that may persist for many years after diagnosis. Periodic screening and referral to mental health professionals if needed are recommended per NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship [BINV-17]. Financial advocacy services should be offered to address financial concerns, employment issues, and family support [BINV-28]. For patients with sexual health concerns or fertility and birth control needs in premenopausal patients, refer to BINV-C.
supportive_care.dental_care: Patients treated with a bisphosphonate or denosumab should undergo a dental examination with preventive dentistry prior to the initiation of therapy [DCIS-2 footnote q, BINV-21 footnote ppp]. During bisphosphonate or denosumab treatment, avoid invasive dental procedures if possible to reduce risk of osteonecrosis of the jaw [493].

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Post-therapy follow-up is optimally performed by members of the treatment team and includes the performance of regular history/physical examinations every 4 to 6 months for the first 5 years after primary therapy and annually thereafter. Mammography should be performed annually. For patients treated with BCT and RT, annual mammograms are the appropriate frequency for surveillance, with no clear advantage to shorter interval imaging. Patients should wait 6 to 12 months after the completion of RT to begin their annual mammogram surveillance [BINV-17]. Suspicious findings on physical examination or surveillance imaging should warrant immediate follow-up/workup. The Panel notes that routine imaging of reconstructed breast is not indicated after mastectomy but diagnostic imaging may be required for new symptomatic concerns [BINV-H 5 of 7].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: In the absence of clinical signs and symptoms suggestive of recurrent disease, laboratory or imaging studies to screen for metastasis are not necessary. The routine performance of alkaline phosphatase tests and LFTs are not included in the guidelines [429-431]. There is no evidence to support the use of 'tumor markers' for breast cancer, and routine bone scans, CT scans, MRI scans, PET scans, or ultrasound examinations in the asymptomatic patient provide no advantage in survival or ability to palliate recurrent disease [110,432]. Studies indicate that annual mammograms are the appropriate frequency for surveillance of patients with breast cancer who have had BCS and RT with no clear advantage to shorter interval imaging [BINV-17].
late_effects_screening: Cardiotoxicity monitoring for patients who received left-sided RT, anthracyclines, or HER2-targeted therapy [BINV-17]
Bone health monitoring: BMD determination at baseline and periodically for patients on aromatase inhibitors or with ovarian failure secondary to treatment [BINV-17]
Lymphedema screening and referral as needed [BINV-17]

Age-appropriate gynecologic screening for patients on tamoxifen; routine endometrial biopsy is not recommended but rapid evaluation of vaginal spotting is indicated due to risk of tamoxifen-associated endometrial carcinoma [436]
Periodic screening for changes in family history and genetic testing indications [BINV-17]
Cognitive dysfunction screening per NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship [BINV-28]
Psychosocial support including screening for anxiety, fear of progression, depression, and PTSD [BINV-17, BINV-28]
Fertility and sexual health assessment per BINV-C
Menopausal hormone therapy is relatively contraindicated in survivors of hormonally mediated cancers; should be used with caution in those with increased genetic cancer risk [BINV-17]
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: The majority of recurrences of DCIS are in-breast recurrences after BCT, and recurrences mostly occur in close proximity to the location of the prior disease. Overall, approximately one-half of the local recurrences after initial treatment for a pure DCIS are invasive in nature, whereas the remainder recur as pure DCIS [MS-9]. For invasive breast cancer, locoregional recurrences can occur at the breast/chest wall, axillary nodes, or supraclavicular/internal mammary nodes. The risk of recurrence between years 5 and 20 is considerable, highlighting the importance of extended endocrine therapy considerations in HR-positive disease [318]. Rates of contralateral breast cancer are increased in patients with BRCA1/2 mutations when compared with patients with sporadic breast cancer [433-435].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: History and physical exam: every 4-6 months for the first 5 years after primary therapy, then annually thereafter [BINV-17]
Interval history and physical examination: every 6-12 months for 5 years, then annually (for DCIS patients) [DCIS-2]
Gynecologic screening: age-appropriate for patients on tamoxifen; routine annual pelvis ultrasound is not recommended [BINV-17]

Genetic screening: periodic screening for changes in family history and genetic testing indications; referral to genetic counseling as indicated per NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High Risk Assessment [BINV-17]
Bone density assessment: baseline and every 2 years for males on GnRH analog therapy; periodically for all patients on AI or with treatment-induced ovarian failure [BINV-J 1 of 2, BINV-17]
imaging_strategy: Mammography: annually, beginning 6-12 months after completion of RT following BCS [BINV-17]
Routine imaging of reconstructed breast: not indicated after mastectomy; diagnostic imaging for new symptomatic concerns only [BINV-17, BINV-H 5 of 7]
Breast MRI: optional, may be considered in patients with high lifetime risk (>20%) based on models largely dependent on family history [BINV-B, BINV-17]
No routine systemic imaging (CT, bone scan, PET) in the absence of clinical signs/symptoms of metastatic disease [BINV-17]
laboratory_monitoring: No routine laboratory monitoring (CBC, LFTs, tumor markers) in the absence of symptoms suggestive of metastatic disease [BINV-17]
CBC and LFTs considered only if patient is a candidate for systemic therapy [BINV-12 footnote a]
Biomarker testing (ER/PR, HER2) should be performed on all new primary or newly metastatic breast cancers [BINV-A]
supportive_follow_up: Assess and encourage adherence to adjuvant endocrine therapy; implement simple strategies to enhance patient adherence such as direct questioning during office visits [BINV-17]
Monitor for lymphedema with pretreatment baseline measurements and ongoing assessment [BINV-17]
Coordinate care between PCP and oncology specialists; provide personalized survivorship treatment plan [BINV-17]
Screen for distress, psychosocial issues, and provide referrals as needed [BINV-17, BINV-28]
For patients with germline BRCA1/2 mutations: refer to NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High Risk Assessment regarding contralateral risk-reducing mastectomy and enhanced surveillance

special_situations.key_message: Paget disease of the breast is a rare manifestation of breast cancer characterized by neoplastic cells in the epidermis of the nipple-areolar complex (NAC), most commonly presenting with eczema of the areola, bleeding, ulceration, and itching. There is an associated cancer elsewhere in the breast in up to approximately 80%-90% of cases [666-668].
special_situations.management: Complete history and physical examination and diagnostic breast imaging are required. The skin of the NAC should undergo full-thickness surgical biopsy. When biopsy is positive for Paget disease, breast MRI is recommended to define the extent of disease [668,669]. Treatment options include BCS with central lumpectomy including NAC ± SLNB with whole breast RT, or total mastectomy including NAC ± SLNB with or without breast reconstruction. For Paget disease without an associated cancer, removal of the entire NAC with negative margin of underlying breast tissue is recommended. Adjuvant systemic therapy should be administered based on stage and HR status of any underlying cancer [MS-88-89].
special_situations.evidence: No category 1 data specifically address local management of Paget disease. Systemic therapy is supported by evidence cited in stage-specific breast cancer treatment guidelines.

special_situations.key_message: Phyllodes tumors of the breast are rare tumors comprised of both stromal and epithelial elements, existing in benign, borderline, and malignant subtypes. The subtype appears less important for risk of recurrence than the margin of tumor-free resection achieved [677-678].
special_situations.management: Treatment is local surgical excision with tumor-free margins of ≥1 cm. Lumpectomy or partial mastectomy is the preferred surgical therapy. Total mastectomy is necessary only if negative margins cannot be obtained. Axillary lymph node staging is not necessary unless lymph nodes are pathologic on clinical exam [681,682]. No adjuvant cytotoxic chemotherapy or endocrine therapy provides proven benefit in reduction of recurrences or death. For patients with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (germline TP53 mutation), there is an increased risk for phyllodes tumors [680]. Local recurrences are the most common site of recurrence; most distant recurrences occur in the lung.
special_situations.evidence: Management based on consensus and retrospective data. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma for systemic treatment of distant recurrences.

special_situations.key_message: Breast cancer occurring concurrently with pregnancy is infrequent but is most often node-positive with larger primary tumors, poorly differentiated histology, more frequently ER/PR-negative, and approximately 30% HER2-positive. Diagnosis is often delayed [685-687].
special_situations.management: Staging should be tailored to minimize fetal exposure: CT scans and nuclear imaging (bone scan or PET/CT) and contrast-enhanced MRI are contraindicated during pregnancy [PREG-1]. Chemotherapy should not be administered during the first trimester. RT should not be administered during any trimester. Anthracycline and alkylating agent chemotherapy appears safe during second and third trimesters. Preferred regimen is AC (doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide) every 3 weeks or weekly paclitaxel. Anti-HER2 therapies (trastuzumab, pertuzumab, T-DM1), CDK4/6 inhibitors, PARP inhibitors, immune checkpoint inhibitors, and endocrine therapy are contraindicated during pregnancy [PREG-2]. Surgical options include mastectomy or BCS if RT can be delayed to postpartum period [689,690].
special_situations.evidence: Data primarily from retrospective studies and small prospective studies. FAC chemotherapy may be given with relative safety during second and third trimesters [697,698].

special_situations.key_message: IBC is a rare, aggressive form of breast cancer estimated to account for 1%-6% of breast cancer cases. It is characterized by rapid onset of erythema, edema, and peau d'orange occupying ≥1/3 of the breast. It is usually HR-negative and more frequently HER2-positive than usual ductal cancers. Pathologic confirmation of invasive breast cancer is required, but dermal lymphatic involvement is neither required nor sufficient alone for diagnosis [715-722].
special_situations.management: Requires combined modality approach: preoperative systemic therapy with anthracycline-based regimen ± taxanes followed by mastectomy (skin-sparing and nipple-sparing mastectomy are contraindicated) with level I/II ALND, then RT to chest wall and comprehensive RNI including any portion of undissected axilla at risk ± delayed breast reconstruction [IBC-2]. BCs is not recommended. For HER2-positive disease, trastuzumab should be included; a pertuzumab-containing regimen may be administered preoperatively. All patients with HR-positive IBC should receive endocrine therapy sequentially after completing planned preoperative systemic therapy.
special_situations.evidence: Treatment recommendations based on retrospective analyses, small prospective studies, and data from non-IBC locally advanced breast cancer. No large randomized trials specifically evaluating optimal systemic treatment exist [732-743].

special_situations.key_message: Male breast cancer is rare, and few males have been included in breast cancer trials. Management is generally extrapolated from trials in females, though some biologic and clinical differences exist [BINV-J 1 of 2].
special_situations.management: Genetic testing is recommended for all males with breast cancer. BCS is feasible and safe based on evolving data, though mastectomy has historically been more common. SLNB should be performed for clinically node-negative axilla. Adjuvant endocrine therapy options include tamoxifen for 5-10 years; if tamoxifen is contraindicated, GnRH analog plus aromatase inhibitor. Single-agent aromatase inhibitor is not recommended due to inferior outcomes compared to tamoxifen [BINV-J 1 of 2].
special_situations.evidence: Most recommendations extrapolated from female breast cancer trials. Available data support 21-gene assay recurrence score provides prognostic information in males [12,13].

special_situations.key_message: About 5% of all breast cancer patients carry germline BRCA pathogenic variants, with higher rates in HER2-negative disease [642]. In the metastatic setting, germline BRCA1/2 PVs should be assessed in all patients with recurrent or metastatic breast cancer to identify candidates for PARP inhibitor therapy.
special_situations.management: In the adjuvant setting for germline BRCA1/2 PV carriers with high-risk HER2-negative disease, adjuvant olaparib for 1 year is recommended (category 1) based on the OlympiA trial showing 4-year OS of 89.8% with olaparib vs 86.4% with placebo [373]. In the metastatic setting, olaparib and talazoparib are category 1, preferred options based on OlympiAD (PFS 7.0 vs 4.2 months; HR 0.58) [643] and EMBRACA trials (PFS 8.6 vs 5.6 months; HR 0.54) [645]. Platinum agents (carboplatin, cisplatin) are also preferred for TNBC with germline BRCA1/2 PVs. For germline PALB2 PVs, olaparib is listed as an option based on phase II data showing ORR of 75% and median PFS of 9.4 months [647,648].
special_situations.evidence: Category 1 recommendations based on OlympiA (adjuvant) [373], OlympiAD [643], and EMBRACA [645] phase III trials.

special_situations.key_message: Optimal management of oligometastatic stage IV breast cancers remains uncertain. Routine utilization of locoregional treatment of the primary tumor and ablative treatment for metastatic sites have not been demonstrated to improve survival outcomes in prospective trials, however, may reduce the risk of local progression and palliate symptoms [BINV-18 footnote kkk].
special_situations.management: Compared with recurrent oligometastatic disease, patients with de novo oligometastatic cancer have better outcomes and therapeutic decisions should be individualized and discussed in a multidisciplinary context. For de novo oligometastatic disease with favorable features (HER2+ or ER+, induced NED status after optimal multi-agent systemic regimens, limited sites of metastases, bone-only metastases, and young age/longevity), an aggressive multimodality approach involving locoregional therapy of the primary tumor, ablative treatment to all sites of metastases, and 'adjuvant-like' systemic therapy may offer improved long-term PFS and may be considered.
special_situations.evidence: A large number of case series document long-term DFS for patients with de novo oligometastatic breast cancer who received multimodality therapy leading to 'no evidence of disease' (NED) status. Randomized data do not demonstrate survival benefit of locoregional treatment in this setting.

special_situations.key_message: DCIS-M, defined as an invasive focus ≤1 mm in size, should refer to the DCIS margin definition (≥2 mm) when considering optimal margin width, given that the majority of DCIS-M is comprised of DCIS and systemic therapy utilization for this lesion more closely reflects the treatment pattern for DCIS than for invasive carcinoma [BINV-F footnote, MS-7].
special_situations.management: Management follows DCIS guidelines for local therapy. Surgical excision is recommended. Margin width of at least 2 mm is recommended. When considering axillary staging, DCIS-M should be managed similarly to pure DCIS, though SLNB may be considered in select circumstances [BINV-F].
special_situations.evidence: Based on the clinical behavior of DCIS-M being more similar to DCIS than invasive carcinoma in terms of natural history and treatment patterns.

clinical_pearls: Breast cancer is stratified for adjuvant therapy based on HR status and HER2 expression. Patients with favorable histologic types (pure tubular, mucinous, cribriform, adenoid cystic, secretory carcinoma) should have the favorable histologic type confirmed as not high grade, pure (>90%), and HER2-negative to qualify for reduced adjuvant therapy [BINV-4 footnote cc].

For premenopausal patients with HR-positive, HER2-negative disease, 5 years of OFS combined with tamoxifen or an aromatase inhibitor is recommended for those at higher risk of recurrence based on SOFT and TEXT trial data showing the 15-year DFS with OFS + exemestane was 73.5% vs 67.0% for tamoxifen alone [314-316].

The 21-gene assay (Oncotype DX) is the only multigene panel validated as both prognostic AND predictive for chemotherapy benefit. Other prognostic assays (70-gene, 50-gene, 12-gene) provide prognostic information but have not been validated to predict chemotherapy benefit [BINV-N 1 of 5].

Achieving pathologic complete response (pCR) to preoperative systemic therapy is associated with favorable prognosis, with the correlation being strongest for TNBC, somewhat less so for HER2-positive disease, and least for ER-positive disease [256,257].

In patients receiving preoperative systemic therapy for node-positive disease (cN+), SLNB has a >10% false-negative rate. This can be improved by marking the most suspicious biopsied node prior to treatment, using dual tracers, obtaining ≥3 sentinel lymph nodes, and removal of the marked node (Targeted Axillary Dissection [TAD]) [BINV-14 footnote xx].

For HR-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer, endocrine therapy plus CDK4/6 inhibitor is preferred over chemotherapy, even for extensive visceral involvement. Chemotherapy is reserved for true visceral crisis defined as 'severe organ dysfunction... implying important organ compromise leading to a clinical indication for the most rapidly efficacious therapy' [494, BINV-22].

fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is associated with interstitial lung disease (ILD)/pneumonitis, and regular monitoring is recommended. For patients with a history of ILD/pneumonitis, there are no data on managing safety or toxicity of this drug in a clinical trial [BINV-Q footnote o].

Germline BRCA1/2 pathogenic variants should be assessed in all patients with recurrent or metastatic breast cancer to identify candidates for PARP inhibitor therapy. While olaparib and talazoparib are FDA-indicated in HER2-negative disease, the NCCN Panel supports use in any breast cancer subtype associated with germline BRCA1/2 mutations [BINV-Q 7 of 15 footnote].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Breast Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Version 5.2026, last updated July 7, 2026. Category 2A recommendations unless otherwise indicated. All recommendations are considered appropriate.

guidelines_resources.organization: American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO)/College of American Pathologists (CAP)
guidelines_resources.title: HER2 Testing in Breast Cancer Guideline
guidelines_resources.notes: NCCN endorses the ASCO/CAP HER2 testing guideline for methodology of HER2 testing on all new primary or newly metastatic breast cancers [BINV-A].

guidelines_resources.title: Estrogen and Progesterone Receptor Testing in Breast Cancer Guideline Update
guidelines_resources.notes: HR testing (ER and PR) by IHC should be performed on any new primary or newly metastatic breast cancer using methodology outlined in the latest ASCO/CAP HR testing guideline [5, BINV-A 4 of 4].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for risk criteria for hereditary breast cancer, genetic counseling recommendations, and risk reduction strategies [BINV-1].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer Screening and Diagnosis
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for indications for screening MRI in patients at increased breast cancer risk [BINV-B].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A)
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health, for assessing patient distress [DCIS-1 footnote f].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for management of lymphedema, cardiovascular disease risk assessment, fertility and sexual health concerns, and long-term survivorship issues [BINV-17, BINV-28].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for G-CSF support during dose-dense chemotherapy regimens [BINV-M footnote n].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for screening and management of immunotherapy-related toxicities [BINV-M 1 of 10].

guidelines_resources.title: Accelerated Partial Breast Irradiation (APBI) Consensus Statement
guidelines_resources.notes: The NCCN Panel endorses the updated ASTRO APBI consensus statement for guidance on APBI use, including the 2024 criteria [BINV-I 3 of 3].

guidelines_resources.title: Cancer Statistics
guidelines_resources.notes: Estimated 313,510 new breast cancer diagnoses and 42,780 deaths in the US in 2024 [1,2].

guidelines_resources.title: International Consensus Guidelines for Advanced Breast Cancer (ABC 5-7)
guidelines_resources.notes: Defines visceral crisis as 'severe organ dysfunction... implying important organ compromise leading to a clinical indication for the most rapidly efficacious therapy' [494,495].

key_trials.full_name: Trial Assigning Individualized Options for Treatment
key_trials.population: Postmenopausal patients with node-negative, HR-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer and intermediate recurrence score (11-25)
key_trials.intervention: Endocrine therapy alone vs chemotherapy followed by endocrine therapy
key_trials.key_result: Similar disease-free survival rates at 9 years in those who received adjuvant chemotherapy followed by endocrine therapy compared with endocrine therapy alone. In subset analysis, patients 50 years or younger with RS 16-25 had lower rates of distant recurrence with addition of chemotherapy.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No significant difference in OS between treatment arms
key_trials.practice_change: Postmenopausal patients with node-negative, HR-positive, HER2-negative tumors and intermediate recurrence scores (11-25) may omit chemotherapy. Premenopausal patients with RS 16-25 may benefit from chemotherapy.

key_trials.full_name: SWOG S1007
key_trials.population: Patients with 1-3 lymph node-positive, HR-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer and a recurrence score ≤25
key_trials.intervention: Standard endocrine therapy with or without adjuvant chemotherapy
key_trials.key_result: Addition of adjuvant chemotherapy to endocrine therapy improved invasive disease-free survival among premenopausal—but not postmenopausal—women
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No significant OS benefit with chemotherapy in the overall population
key_trials.practice_change: Premenopausal patients with node-positive, HR-positive, HER2-negative disease and recurrence score up to 25 benefit from chemotherapy; postmenopausal patients do not

key_trials.full_name: Microarray In Node-negative Disease may Avoid Chemotherapy
key_trials.population: Early breast cancer patients with clinical risk assessment and genomic risk assessment by 70-gene assay (MammaPrint)
key_trials.intervention: Chemotherapy for patients with high clinical risk/low genomic risk
key_trials.comparator: No chemotherapy for patients with high clinical risk/low genomic risk
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Survival without distant metastases at 5 years
key_trials.key_result: Among those with high clinical risk/low genomic risk, 5-year rate of survival without distant metastasis in those who did not receive chemotherapy was 94.7% (95% CI, 92.5 to 96.2)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Similar outcomes in discordant low clinical risk/high genomic risk group with or without chemotherapy
key_trials.practice_change: 70-gene assay can identify subset of patients with low likelihood of distant recurrence despite high-risk clinical features

key_trials.full_name: CLinician EOs PaTient HeR2+ Advanced breast cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer (approximately 10% previously received trastuzumab in adjuvant/neoadjuvant setting)
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 18.5 vs 12.4 months (HR 0.62; 95% CI, 0.51-0.75; P < .001); 34% reduction in risk of death (HR 0.66; 95% CI, 0.52-0.84; P = .0008)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS benefit confirmed at 30-month follow-up
key_trials.practice_change: Established pertuzumab + trastuzumab + docetaxel as preferred first-line regimen for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer

key_trials.full_name: MonarchE of Letrozole + Ribociclib in Postmenopausal Advanced Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Postmenopausal patients with HR-positive, HER2-negative recurrent/stage IV breast cancer
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR 43% vs 29%
key_trials.practice_change: Established ribociclib + letrozole as preferred first-line option with OS benefit

key_trials.full_name: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in Previously Treated HER2-Low Advanced Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with HER2-low (HER2 IHC 1+ or 2+/ISH negative) metastatic breast cancer who had received 1-2 prior chemotherapy regimens
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 9.9 vs 5.1 months (HR 0.50); median OS 23 vs 17 months (HR 0.69). Among HR-positive subgroup: PFS 10.1 vs 5.4 months (HR 0.51), OS 23.9 vs 17.6 months (HR 0.69)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade ≥3 adverse events 53% vs 67%; ILD/pneumonitis 12%
key_trials.practice_change: Established fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki as category 1 preferred second-line option for HER2-low metastatic breast cancer

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab plus Chemotherapy vs Placebo plus Chemotherapy in Previously Untreated Locally Recurrent Inoperable or Metastatic TNBC
key_trials.population: Patients with locally recurrent, inoperable, or metastatic TNBC and disease-free interval ≥6 months
key_trials.key_result: In patients with CPS ≥10: median PFS 9.7 vs 5.6 months (HR 0.65; 95% CI, 0.49-0.86); OS 23.0 vs 16.1 months (HR 0.73; 95% CI, 0.55-0.95)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3-4 adverse events comparable between groups
key_trials.practice_change: Established chemotherapy + pembrolizumab as preferred first-line regimen for PD-L1 CPS ≥10 TNBC

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Olaparib for Patients with BRCA1- or BRCA2-Mutated Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with germline BRCA1/2 pathogenic variants and high-risk HER2-negative early breast cancer
key_trials.intervention: Adjuvant olaparib 300 mg PO BID for 1 year
key_trials.practice_change: Established adjuvant olaparib for 1 year as category 1 preferred option for germline BRCA1/2 PV carriers with high-risk HER2-negative disease

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab or Placebo plus Chemotherapy followed by Pembrolizumab or Placebo for Early-Stage TNBC
key_trials.population: Patients with previously untreated stage II-III TNBC
key_trials.intervention: Preoperative carboplatin/paclitaxel + pembrolizumab followed by AC/EC + pembrolizumab, then adjuvant pembrolizumab
key_trials.comparator: Preoperative chemotherapy + placebo followed by AC/EC + placebo, then adjuvant placebo
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Among pCR patients, 5-year EFS 92.2% vs 88.2%
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab-containing preoperative regimen followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab as standard for stage II-III TNBC

key_trials.full_name: Elacestrant vs Standard Endocrine Therapy for ER+/HER2- Metastatic Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with ER-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer who received at least one prior line of endocrine therapy and progressed on CDK4/6 inhibitor (72% had ESR1-mutated tumors)
key_trials.comparator: Standard endocrine therapy (fulvestrant or aromatase inhibitor)
key_trials.key_result: Overall median PFS 2.79 vs 1.9 months (HR 0.69; P = .0018). In subgroup with prior ET+CDK4/6i ≥12 months: median PFS 8.6 vs 1.9 months (HR 0.41; 95% CI, 0.26-0.63) [528,529]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Nausea (35% vs 19%), vomiting (19% vs 9%)
key_trials.practice_change: Established elacestrant as an option after prior AI with CDK4/6 inhibitor therapy, particularly in ESR1-mutated tumors

key_trials.full_name: Everolimus in Postmenopausal Hormone-Receptor-Positive Advanced Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Postmenopausal patients with HR-positive advanced breast cancer that had disease progression during treatment with a nonsteroidal AI
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Stomatitis, infections, rash, pneumonitis, and hyperglycemia more frequent with everolimus
key_trials.practice_change: Established everolimus + exemestane as an option for patients who fulfill BOLERO-2 criteria (progressed within 12 months or on nonsteroidal AI)

key_trials.full_name: Accelerated Partial Breast Irradiation using Intensity-Modulated Radiotherapy versus Whole Breast Irradiation
key_trials.population: Patients with early-stage breast cancer treated with BCS
key_trials.intervention: APBI using IMRT (30 Gy in 5 fractions every other day)
key_trials.key_result: 5-year OS comparable between groups. APBI produced less acute and late toxicity and better cosmetic outcomes at 10-year follow-up [187]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Local control equivalent
key_trials.practice_change: Supports APBI as an equivalent alternative to WBRT for selected low-risk patients

key_trials.full_name: Hypofractionated Breast Radiotherapy for 1 Week versus 3 Weeks
key_trials.population: Patients with non-metastatic breast cancer after BCS or mastectomy
key_trials.intervention: 27 Gy in 5 fractions over 1 week or 26 Gy in 5 fractions over 1 week
key_trials.key_result: 5-year IBTR: 2.1% with 40 Gy vs 1.7% with 27 Gy (HR 0.86; 95% CI, 0.51-1.44) and 1.4% with 26 Gy (HR 0.67; 95% CI, 0.38-1.16). Moderate/marked tissue effects: 15% with 27 Gy, 12% with 26 Gy, 10% with 40 Gy [176]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Toxicity comparable between regimens
key_trials.practice_change: Established ultra-hypofractionated regimens (26-27 Gy in 5 fractions) as acceptable alternatives to conventional hypofractionation

key_trials.full_name: Prospective Randomized Trial for Good-Risk DCIS Comparing Radiotherapy with Observation
key_trials.population: Patients with low-risk DCIS (low- to intermediate-grade, <2.5 cm, margins ≥3 mm)
key_trials.key_result: Reduced risk of local recurrence with RT vs observation (0.9% vs 6.7%; HR 0.11; 95% CI, 0.03-0.47) [24]. With 15-year follow-up, local recurrence rates reduced by 50% with RT vs without RT (7.1% vs 15.1%; HR 0.36; 95% CI, 0.20-0.66) [42]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No difference in DFS or OS
key_trials.practice_change: Supports omission of RT in carefully selected low-risk DCIS patients meeting all RTOG 9804 criteria (screen-detected, grade 1-2, size ≤2.5 cm, margins ≥3 mm)

key_trials.full_name: Axillary Dissection vs No Axillary Dissection in Women with Sentinel Node Metastasis
key_trials.journal: Journal of the American Medical Association
key_trials.population: Patients ≥18 years with clinical T1/T2 tumors, fewer than 3 positive SLNs, undergoing BCS and WBRT
key_trials.key_result: No difference in OS, DFS, or local recurrence between groups. At 10-year follow-up, cumulative incidence of ipsilateral axillary recurrences: 0.5% with ALND vs 1.5% with SLNB alone (P = .28) [150-152]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Locoregional recurrence at 10 years: 6.2% with ALND vs 5.3% with SLNB (P = .36)
key_trials.practice_change: Established that ALND is not needed for patients with cT1-2, cN0, 1-2 positive SLNs undergoing BCS + WBRT

key_trials.full_name: Abemaciclib Combined with Endocrine Therapy for Adjuvant Treatment of HR+/HER2-, Node-Positive, High-Risk Early Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with HR-positive/HER2-negative, high-risk breast cancer (≥4 positive nodes, or 1-3 positive nodes with grade 3 or tumor ≥5 cm)
key_trials.intervention: Adjuvant abemaciclib 150 mg PO BID for 2 years + endocrine therapy
key_trials.key_result: 4-year iDFS: absolute risk reduction of 6.4% (HR 0.664; 95% CI, 0.578-0.762; P < .0001) [369]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Consistent benefit across predefined subgroups
key_trials.practice_change: Established 2 years of adjuvant abemaciclib + endocrine therapy as category 1 preferred option for high-risk HR-positive/HER2-negative early breast cancer

key_trials.full_name: Ribociclib plus Endocrine Therapy in Early Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with stage II-III HR-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer
key_trials.intervention: Ribociclib 400 mg PO daily (days 1-21 every 28 days for 3 years) + endocrine therapy
key_trials.key_result: Median follow-up 34 months showed statistically significant improvement (3.3%) in iDFS (HR 0.75; 95% CI, 0.62-0.91; P = .003) [372]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Additional follow-up needed for long-term efficacy characterization
key_trials.practice_change: Established ribociclib + endocrine therapy as category 1 option for stage II-III HR-positive/HER2-negative breast cancer

key_trials.full_name: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan after Endocrine Therapy in Metastatic Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with HR-positive, HER2-low (IHC 1+ or 2+/ISH negative) or HER2-ultralow (IHC 0+) metastatic breast cancer, previously treated with at least one line of endocrine therapy, no prior chemotherapy for metastatic disease
key_trials.key_result: HER2-low subgroup: median PFS 13.2 vs 8.1 months (HR 0.62; 95% CI, 0.51-0.71). HER2-ultralow subgroup: PFS 13.2 vs 8.3 months (HR 0.78; 95% CI, 0.50-1.21) [560]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade ≥3 adverse events 53% vs 44%; ILD/pneumonitis 11% (0.7% fatal)
key_trials.practice_change: Extended fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki indication to HER2-ultralow patients; positioned as category 2A first-line option for endocrine-refractory HR-positive disease

key_trials.full_name: Inavolisib-Based Therapy in PIK3CA-Mutated Advanced Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with PIK3CA-activating mutations with recurrence on or within 12 months of adjuvant endocrine therapy
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 15.0 vs 7.3 months (HR 0.43; 95% CI, 0.32-0.59). Median OS 34 vs 27 months (HR 0.67; 95% CI, 0.48-0.94) [510,511]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Higher rates of hyperglycemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia with inavolisib
key_trials.practice_change: Established fulvestrant/inavolisib/palbociclib as category 1 preferred first-line option for PIK3CA-mutated, HR-positive, endocrine-refractory disease

key_trials.full_name: Datopotamab Deruxtecan in Untreated Advanced Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with PD-L1 CPS <10 or not a candidate for PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy and no germline BRCA1/2 PV with untreated metastatic TNBC
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ≥Grade 3 toxicities 33% vs 29%
key_trials.practice_change: Established datopotamab deruxtecan-dlnk as category 1 preferred first-line option for PD-L1 CPS <10 TNBC without germline BRCA1/2 PV

key_trials.full_name: Sacituzumab Govitecan in Untreated Advanced Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with untreated advanced TNBC
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 9.7 vs 6.9 months (HR 0.62; P < .001). PFS2 14 vs 18.2 months (HR 0.70; 95% CI, 0.55-0.90) [600]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Response duration 12.2 vs 7.2 months; treatment discontinuation 4% vs 12%
key_trials.practice_change: Established sacituzumab govitecan-hziy as category 1 preferred first-line option for PD-L1 CPS <10 TNBC without germline BRCA1/2 PV
